title: WHO donates US$331,000 worth of medical equipment to improve maternal and women’s cancer care in Kenya
summary: The donation includes 108 electric obstetric theatre beds, along with specialised equipment for women’s cancer screening, diagnosis, and treatment.
thbAction: Pitch THB's diagnostics CRM to help labs manage doctor outreach, collection-center expansion, and B2B referral relationships while improving conversion and retention.
contentHtml:
KENYA—The Ministry of Health has received medical equipment worth KSh 43 million (US$331,000) from the World Health Organization (WHO) to strengthen maternal, newborn, and women’s health services across Kenya.

The donation includes 108 electric obstetric theatre beds, along with specialised equipment for women’s cancer screening, diagnosis, and treatment.

The handover ceremony took place at Kenyatta National Hospital and was led by WHO representative Dr. Neema Rusibamayila Kimambo.

The equipment was officially received by the Principal Secretary for Medical Services, Dr. Ouma Oluga, who also oversaw its distribution to beneficiary counties and health facilities.

Distribution to priority counties and health facilities

The 108 electric obstetric theatre beds will be deployed to 45 health facilities across 11 priority counties.

These beds are expected to improve emergency obstetric care and enhance the management of childbirth complications, particularly in high-burden regions.

In addition, cancer screening and treatment equipment will be supplied to eight health facilities in Bungoma and Nyandarua counties.

The package includes diagnostic, laboratory, pathology, and treatment tools designed to strengthen early detection and management of cervical and breast cancer.

Strengthening maternal health under EWENE Plan

Speaking during the event, Dr. Oluga said the donation aligns with government efforts under the Every Woman Every Newborn Everywhere (EWENE) Acceleration Plan, which was launched by President William Ruto in May 2026.

He noted that the initiative focuses on reducing preventable maternal and newborn deaths by improving access to quality care services at all levels of the health system.

He added that continued investment in maternal health infrastructure remains critical to advancing Universal Health Coverage and ensuring safer childbirth outcomes across the country.

Expanding cancer screening and early detection

The Principal Secretary also highlighted ongoing progress under the Women’s Integrated Cancer Services Project, implemented in partnership with WHO and Roche.

The programme integrates breast and cervical cancer screening into routine primary healthcare services, making it easier for women to access early diagnosis.

To date, 8,440 women have been screened for breast and cervical cancers as well as selected non-communicable diseases.

With the arrival of the new equipment, the Ministry expects to expand screening, diagnostic capacity, and timely treatment services in underserved regions.

Accountability and Health System Strengthening

Dr. Oluga urged counties and health facilities receiving the equipment to ensure proper utilisation, maintenance, and accountability to maximise its impact.

He emphasized that efficient use of the resources would help improve service delivery and patient outcomes.

The Ministry of Health reiterated its commitment to working closely with county governments, development partners, and healthcare workers to strengthen maternal, newborn, and women’s health services nationwide.

It also noted ongoing efforts to modernise health systems and expand access to essential care in line with broader national health reforms.
